title: Child Custody Laws in Bangladesh: A Complete Legal Guide
metaTitle: Child Custody Laws in Bangladesh – Complete Legal Guide 2026
metaDescription: How child custody works in Bangladesh — hizanat (mother's custody rights), father's guardianship, Family Court process, and how to protect your child's welfare. Expert guide from a family lawyer in Dhaka.
heroIntro: When a marriage breaks down, the wellbeing of children becomes the most urgent concern. Bangladesh's child custody law balances the mother's primary custody rights (hizanat) with the father's role as natural guardian — and places the child's welfare above all else.
toc: The Fundamental Principle: Child's Welfare First
When Does Custody Transfer from Mother to Father?
How the Family Court Decides Custody
Visitation Rights for the Non-Custodial Parent
Taking a Child Abroad: What the Law Says
How to Apply for Custody in Family Court

sections.h2: The Fundamental Principle: Child's Welfare First
sections.content:
Bangladesh courts — applying both Islamic personal law principles and the Guardians and Wards Act 1890 — make child custody decisions based primarily on the welfare of the child. No parent has an absolute right that overrides the child's best interests.

This welfare principle considers the child's physical safety, emotional security, educational needs, religious upbringing, and continuity of care. If you are facing a custody dispute, a family lawyer in Dhaka can assess your situation and advise on how courts in your jurisdiction typically apply these principles.

Under Islamic personal law as applied in Bangladesh, the mother has the presumptive right to hizanat (physical custody) of young children. Age thresholds under traditional Islamic jurisprudence are:

  • Sons: Mother retains hizanat until the boy reaches approximately 7 years of age
  • Daughters: Mother retains hizanat until puberty

After these ages, physical custody may transfer to the father, but Bangladesh courts have increasingly applied the welfare principle to extend maternal custody where it serves the child's interests. Divorce does not end the mother's hizanat rights — the mother retains custody even after divorce until the above thresholds are met, unless the court orders otherwise.

While the mother typically has primary physical custody (hizanat), the father is the natural legal guardian of the child under Muslim personal law. This means the father:

  • Controls major decisions affecting the child (education institution, major medical decisions, passport applications)
  • Is responsible for the child's financial maintenance regardless of who has custody
  • Has the right to regular visitation and contact with the child

Both parents have distinct but complementary roles. Disputes arise when parents cannot agree on exercise of these roles — in which case the Family Court intervenes.

sections.h2: When Does Custody Transfer from Mother to Father?
sections.content:
The mother's hizanat right can be lost or limited if she:

  • Remarries a man who is not the child's mahram (a close relative)
  • Is found unfit to care for the child (due to serious illness, addiction, neglect, or abuse)
  • Lives far away such that the child's access to education or the father's visitation is unreasonably restricted
  • Voluntarily gives up custody

Even if the mother loses hizanat, the child's welfare remains paramount. Courts will not simply transfer custody to the father if that also does not serve the child. Grandparents or other relatives may be considered if neither parent is suitable.

sections.h2: How the Family Court Decides Custody
sections.content:
Custody cases are heard by the Family Court under the Family Courts Ordinance 1985. The court considers:

  • Each parent's ability to provide financially, emotionally, and educationally for the child
  • The child's existing attachment and relationship with each parent
  • Any history of domestic violence, abuse, or neglect by either parent
  • The child's own preference (given weight in proportion to the child's age and maturity)
  • Continuity of the child's schooling, social life, and community

Courts can and do interview children in chambers. They may also appoint a welfare officer to investigate the circumstances of both parents' homes.

During a pending custody case, either parent can apply for an interim custody order. This determines where the child lives while the main case is proceeding — which can take months or years. Courts grant interim custody quickly to prevent one parent from unilaterally removing the child or denying the other parent access.

Apply for an interim order immediately upon filing the custody suit — do not allow the situation to be unresolved for long periods, as prolonged status quo tends to influence the final order.

sections.h2: Visitation Rights for the Non-Custodial Parent
sections.content:
Whichever parent does not have primary custody retains visitation (access) rights unless the court finds that contact would genuinely harm the child. A typical court-ordered access arrangement might include:

  • Alternate weekend visits
  • Shared school holidays
  • Phone/video call access on specified days
  • Supervised visits (in cases with a history of concern)

Denying the other parent their court-ordered access without good reason is contempt of court — a serious legal offence. Keep records if access is being blocked and discuss with your family lawyer immediately.

sections.h2: Taking a Child Abroad: What the Law Says
sections.content:
Neither parent can take a child out of Bangladesh without either the other parent's written consent or a court order permitting travel. Taking a child abroad without permission — even to visit relatives — constitutes international child abduction under Bangladesh law and may engage international legal frameworks.

If you believe your child may be taken abroad without your consent, apply urgently to the Family Court for an order restricting the child from being removed from Bangladesh. Courts can act within hours in genuine emergency situations.

sections.h2: How to Apply for Custody in Family Court
sections.content:
Steps to apply for child custody in Bangladesh:

  1. Engage a family lawyer who specialises in custody cases.
  2. File a custody application (plaint/petition) at the Family Court in the district where the child resides.
  3. Apply simultaneously for an interim custody order.
  4. Gather evidence: the child's school records, medical records, photographs of your home, character witnesses, and any evidence of the other parent's unfitness (if applicable).
  5. Attend all court hearings — absence without excuse reflects poorly.
  6. Cooperate fully with any welfare officer investigation ordered by the court.

Never deny the other parent access in violation of court orders while your case is pending — it will harm your credibility. Contact Advocate Md. Shah Alam, an experienced family lawyer in Dhaka and Uttara, for immediate custody case assistance.

faqs.question: Does the mother always get custody in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: The mother has a strong presumptive right to custody (hizanat) for young children, but this is not absolute. The court's primary consideration is always the child's welfare, and custody can be granted to the father in appropriate circumstances.

faqs.question: At what age can a child choose which parent to live with in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: There is no fixed age, but courts give increasing weight to the child's preference as they grow older and mature. A teenager's strong preference will carry significant weight in a custody determination.

faqs.question: Can grandparents get custody of a child in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Yes. Courts may grant custody to third parties (including grandparents) if neither parent is fit to care for the child. The welfare principle allows for this flexibility.

faqs.question: What if the father does not pay child maintenance?
faqs.answer: The Family Court can order specific monthly maintenance amounts. Non-payment of court-ordered maintenance is enforceable by contempt of court, which can lead to the father's arrest.

faqs.question: Can I take my child to visit relatives in another country?
faqs.answer: Only with the other parent's written consent or a court order. If the other parent is refusing unreasonably, apply to the court for permission to travel.

faqs.question: What if the custodial parent is abusing the child?
faqs.answer: This is an emergency. File an urgent application with the Family Court for immediate change of custody. Simultaneously report the abuse to the police. Courts act swiftly when there is credible evidence of child abuse.

faqs.question: Does a mother lose custody if she works outside the home?
faqs.answer: No. A mother working outside the home is not a ground for losing custody. Courts look at the overall care arrangement, including who cares for the child when the mother is not home.
